summary: Jeff Atwood examines the emerging dual-core CPU market in 2005, comparing AMD's Athlon 64 X2 against Intel's Pentium 4 D. While celebrating the hardware advances, he warns that multi-core architectures shift the performance burden from hardware to software developers. Single-threaded applications won't automatically benefit from additional cores, meaning programmers must learn to parallelize their code. He predicts four-core CPUs will follow quickly and that eventually throwing money at hardware won't solve performance problems—only rewriting algorithms for parallelism will.
key_points: AMD's Athlon 64 X2 benefits more from dual-core than Pentium 4 due to its on-die memory controller architecture
Single-threaded applications designed for single processors will not benefit from multiple cores without rewriting
Intel's Hyperthreading prepared the market for true dual-core designs
AMD's CEO suggests heterogeneous cores (different core types) as one possible future direction
The challenge is shifting from manufacturing transistors to figuring out what to do with them
money_quotes: Dual core designs are fantastic from a technology standpoint, but as a software developer, it's a scary trend.
If the only way we can increase speed is through extra parallelism (aka threading), our coding and debugging burden just went through the roof.

A single threaded (client) app that has been designed with a single processor and a single thread of execution in mind, will not benefit and therefore users will not benefit from multiple processors or multiple cores.
Making transistors is pretty trivial. We can make hundreds of millions of transistors. Figuring out what the hell to do with those transistors is the challenge.
One day, you won't be able to throw money at your hardware to make your app run faster.
You'll have no choice but to pour that money into parallelizing the algorithms inside your app, which is a far more difficult proposition.
key_insight: The multi-core revolution transfers the burden of performance improvement from hardware engineers to software developers, who must now master the difficult art of parallel programming.
